metadata.title: Claude, Allowed.
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 the one accent. Do not retint.
metadata.greeting_note: HAI persona, shortest-form rule. 'Hi' = one word.
metadata.thesis: The ban is a rule about homework. It is not a prophecy about you.
metadata.note: ILLUSTRATE LAW reel: UI at B00 (cold open with RESULT lines), B06 (verdict artifact), B07 (handoff), B08 (outro). Inner beats B01–B05 each use a distinct custom Remotion scene on cream stage. PREDICT at B04. COLD OPEN, SPARK-LINE, HANDOFF, OUTRO laws in force. brutalist-medhavy partial: PREDICT beat required, exercise handoff. House red line: never teaches evading a ban — teaches the ethical path only.

beats.narration_text: … this is Liam, in for Bear. Your school has an AI policy — maybe a ban. Here's what you actually need to know: what the ban covers, where the exemptions live, and what's always been yours regardless of any policy. Three things, clearly.

props.command: my school banned AI — now what?
output: bans cover: graded work submitted without disclosure
bans don't cover: fluency practice on your own time
the safe rule: ask your teacher — get it in writing

beats.narration_text: Most school AI bans say roughly the same thing: don't use AI to do work you're submitting as your own thinking. They're not banning you from understanding AI, from using it on your own time, or from building fluency with it outside of graded assignments. The ban is narrower than the headline.

openItems: fluency practice on your own time

beats.narration_text: Here's where it gets slippery. Nearly every policy has an exception — typically for specific tools, specific use cases, or instructor-approved uses. The trap isn't the ban; it's the vague exception. Students who get caught usually misread the exception, not the rule.

props.heading: Here's where it gets slippery.
props.body: Nearly every policy has an exception — typically for specific tools, specific use cases, or instructor-approved uses. The trap isn't the ban; it's the vague exception. Students who get caught usually misread the exception, not the rule.

beats.narration_text: What's yours regardless of any policy: fluency. You can use Claude on your own time to quiz yourself on course material, to ask for explanations of concepts you don't understand, and to practice applying ideas to scenarios. None of that is submitting AI work. It's using a tool to study.

openItems: practicing prompting on your own time
quizzing yourself on your own material

beats.narration_text: Commit before the verdict: what actually gets students caught for AI violations? Is it the AI use itself, or something else? Think about what a teacher would actually be able to detect — and what the enforcement evidence actually looks like.

props.heading: Commit before the verdict: what actually gets students caught for AI violations?
props.body: Commit before the verdict: what actually gets students caught for AI violations? Is it the AI use itself, or something else? Think about what a teacher would actually be able to detect — and what the enforcement evidence actually looks like.

beats.narration_text: The answer: it's not the use. It's the undisclosed use. Students caught for AI violations almost universally failed to disclose — they submitted work that read as AI-generated without telling the instructor. Disclosure converts the same action from a violation to a conversation.

props.heading: The answer: it's not the use.
props.body: It's the undisclosed use. Students caught for AI violations almost universally failed to disclose — they submitted work that read as AI-generated without telling the instructor. Disclosure converts the same action from a violation to a conversation.

beats.narration_text: The policy collapses to one question: did you tell your teacher? That's it. Allowed AI use is disclosed AI use. Banned AI use is work that presents AI output as your own thinking without disclosure. The line isn't capability; it's transparency.

artifactLines: Bans cover: graded work submitted without disclosure — not fluency, not practice on your own time.
The exemption trap: 'educational purposes' means different things to different teachers — ask first, get it in writing.
What's yours: AI Fluency course (CC, free), home practice, ungraded skill-building — a ban doesn't ban becoming capable.
The one rule: disclose. A disclosed use is a conversation. An undisclosed one is a violation.

beats.narration_text: Your move: don't assume — ask. The handoff prompt takes your actual assignment and your school's actual policy, and has Claude produce a two-paragraph note you can send your instructor asking whether AI assistance is permitted for this work. Paste it.

props.command: Draft me a short, honest email to my teacher asking whether I can use Claude for [describe your specific assignment or subject]. I want to start the right conversation — keep it one paragraph.

beats.narration_text: Claude, Allowed. If this cleared the fog, subscribe. HAI. Liam, in for Bear.
